name: Ancient Trees of Las Majadas Trail
name_original: El Bosque de Las Majadas desde Uña de Quintana — circular por el río Vega
description: Hike a moderate 8.5-mile circular route through the Valle de Vidriales, exploring unique karstic formations and ancient forests.
region_intro: Hiking around Uña De Quintana offers exploration of a diverse landscape in the province of Zamora, Spain. The region features fertile lands nourished by the Tera River and various streams, with predominantly flat terrain. The northeastern section includes low mountainous areas such as "El Monte" and "Pico El Cuerno," reaching approximately 800 meters. This varied topography, along with traditional underground wineries, contributes to a distinct "vallesano" environment. Best hiking trails around Uña De Quintana The most popular hiking route is Bosque Las Majadas y arboles milenarios — vuelta desde San Pedro de Ceque, a 7.5 miles (12.0 km) moderate trail that takes 3 hours 6 minutes to complete, exploring ancient trees and local woodlands. Another top favourite among local hikers is Nogarejas from Pinilla de la Valdería — circular by the Castillo de Nogarejas, a difficult 14.4 miles (23.2 km) path. This route offers a longer challenge, passing by the Castillo de Nogarejas. Local hikers also love the Camino Sanabrés: Olleros de Tera Loop, a 5.4 miles (8.7 km) trail leading through the Tera River valley, often completed in about 2 hours 19 minutes. Hiking around Uña De Quintana is defined by river valleys, low hills, and agricultural lands, with routes often passing historical features like old mills and traditional wineries. The network offers options for moderate ability levels, with one more challenging route available. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 5 hikers have used komoot to explore Uña De Quintana's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Uña De Quintana?
A: Komoot features 5 hiking routes around Uña De Quintana. These trails offer a range of experiences, from moderate walks along river valleys to more challenging paths through the region's varied topography.

Q: What kind of landscape can I expect to see while hiking in Uña De Quintana?
A: Hiking in Uña De Quintana offers a diverse landscape. You'll find fertile lands nourished by the Tera and Regato Rivers, predominantly flat terrain, and low mountainous areas in the northeast, such as "El Monte" and "Pico El Cuerno." The area is also characterized by traditional underground wineries (*bodegas subterráneas*) and historical features like ancient fountains and old mills.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Uña De Quintana?
A: Yes, Uña De Quintana offers several circular routes. For example, you can explore the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1271771438/nogarejas-from-pinilla-de-la-valderia-circular-by-the-castillo-de-nogarejas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Nogarejas from Pinilla de la Valdería — circular by the Castillo de Nogarejas&lt;/a&gt;, which is a 23.2 km loop. Another option is the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1305488286/camino-sanabres-olleros-de-tera-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino Sanabrés: Olleros de Tera Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a shorter 8.7 km trail through the Tera River valley.

Q: What are some interesting landmarks or attractions I can see near the hiking trails?
A: The region around Uña De Quintana is rich in points of interest. You can visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3941370" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monument to the Pilgrim of Rionegro del Puente&lt;/a&gt;, or enjoy the serene views at the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3834108" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Nuestra Señora del Agavanzal Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6112661" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tera River Trail&lt;/a&gt; itself is a notable feature, and you might also encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5452845" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Castrocontrigo&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4461714" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Virgen de la Carballeda Pilgrims' Hostel&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly hiking routes in Uña De Quintana?
A: While there are no routes specifically classified as 'easy' in our current selection for Uña De Quintana, several moderate trails are suitable for those with a reasonable fitness level. These routes generally feature gentle elevation changes and well-defined paths, making them accessible for most hikers. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1305973560/ancient-trees-of-las-majadas-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ancient Trees of Las Majadas Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 13.7 km route.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Uña De Quintana?
A: Given its location in Spain, Uña De Quintana is generally pleasant for hiking during spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October) when temperatures are milder. Summers can be hot, so early mornings or late afternoons are recommended if hiking during July and August. Winters are typically mild but can be damp, especially along the river valleys.

Q: Are the hiking trails in Uña De Quintana suitable for families with children?
A: Many of the moderate trails in Uña De Quintana, with their relatively flat terrain and gentle inclines, can be suitable for families. Routes that follow the river valleys or explore local woodlands, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022496865/bosque-las-majadas-y-arboles-milenarios-vuelta-desde-san-pedro-de-ceque" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bosque Las Majadas y arboles milenarios — vuelta desde San Pedro de Ceque&lt;/a&gt;, offer engaging natural environments for children to explore. Always check the specific route's distance and duration to ensure it matches your family's capabilities.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Uña De Quintana?
A: Uña De Quintana is a rural area with a strong connection to nature, making many of its trails generally dog-friendly. However, it's always advisable to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through agricultural lands, near livestock, or in areas with protected wildlife. Be sure to carry water for your pet and clean up after them.

Q: What are the typical durations for hikes in this region?
A: Hiking durations in Uña De Quintana vary depending on the route's length and difficulty. You can find shorter loops like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1305488286/camino-sanabres-olleros-de-tera-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino Sanabrés: Olleros de Tera Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which takes about 2 hours 19 minutes to complete. Longer, more challenging routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1271771438/nogarejas-from-pinilla-de-la-valderia-circular-by-the-castillo-de-nogarejas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Nogarejas from Pinilla de la Valdería — circular by the Castillo de Nogarejas&lt;/a&gt;, can take over 6 hours.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural points of interest along the trails?
A: Yes, the trails often pass by significant historical and cultural sites. You'll encounter ancient fountains and old mills along the river courses, reflecting the region's past. The distinctive traditional underground wineries (*bodegas subterráneas*) are also a unique feature of the landscape, offering a glimpse into local heritage. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1306768135/petavonium-roman-camp-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Petavonium Roman Camp Trail&lt;/a&gt; explores an area with historical significance.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Uña De Quintana?
A: Hikers often praise the serene rural environment and the diverse natural landscapes of Uña De Quintana. The presence of the Tera River and its tributaries, along with the low mountainous areas, provides a varied and engaging experience. Many appreciate the opportunity to explore traditional features like old mills and underground wineries, which add a unique cultural dimension to the hikes.
tips: Uña de Quintana is a small town in the fertile Vega valley surrounded by a landscape of cereal fields and orchards.
The Majadas de San Pedro de Ceque Forest with ancient and leafy oaks and holm oaks such as the "Los Enamorados" holm oaks, the "La vaciada" holm oak or other hundred-year-old holm oaks.
Nice picture of some oak groves that we find in the Valley of Vidriales.
Along the way, we sighted a traditional adobe corrala near the town of San Pedro de Ceque and the Arroyo del Regato.

faqs.question: What is the estimated hiking time for this route?
faqs.answer: The estimated hiking time for this circular route is approximately 3.5 hours, covering a distance of about 13.7 kilometers. This duration is based on a moderate hiking pace.

faqs.question: Is this a circular hiking route?
faqs.answer: Yes, this is a circular route, meaning you will end your hike at the same point where you started, without needing to retrace your steps.

faqs.question: Where is the trailhead and parking for this hike?
faqs.answer: The trailhead for this route is located near Uña de Quintana. Ample free parking is available at the starting point, making access convenient.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: While the route is generally classified as 'moderate' due to its length and varied terrain, it requires a good level of fitness. Some sections within the larger Callejones de Las Majadas area are considered easier and suitable for families, but this specific route might be challenging for absolute beginners or very young children.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the El Bosque de Las Majadas trail?
faqs.answer: The trail traverses an area known for its dramatic karstic formations, creating a natural labyrinth of narrow corridors, passages, arches, monoliths, and bridges. You'll hike through ravines, gorges, and extensive pine forests, characteristic of the Serranía de Cuenca. The path winds through these impressive rock formations, often likened to a smaller version of the 'Ciudad Encantada'.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife and plants can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: This area is a sanctuary for diverse wildlife, offering opportunities to spot deer, fallow deer, roe deer, wild boar, ibex, and mouflon. Birdwatchers might see various birds of prey like griffon vultures and golden eagles. The forests are primarily composed of black pine, Scots pine, juniper, and oak. In spring, the trails are adorned with wild flowers such as lavender, thyme, rockrose, wild roses, and hawthorn blossoms.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike El Bosque de Las Majadas?
faqs.answer: The landscape transforms significantly with each season, offering a different visual spectacle throughout the year. Spring brings vibrant wildflowers and pleasant temperatures, while autumn showcases beautiful fall foliage. Summer can be warm, but the forest provides shade. Winter offers a unique, serene experience, though conditions might require appropriate gear.

faqs.question: What are the main natural features and landmarks along the route?
faqs.answer: The most notable feature is the passage through the 'Callejones de Las Majadas,' a geological marvel where erosion has carved limestone rocks into fascinating shapes resembling streets, arches, and natural bridges. You'll also pass through the Ancient Trees of Las Majadas and the Oak forests of the Valle de Vidriales.

faqs.question: Does this trail connect with other hiking routes?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route also passes through sections of the Calle Eras trail, covering approximately 4% of its length.

faqs.question: Is the El Bosque de Las Majadas trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, trails in natural parks like this are dog-friendly, but it's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure a pleasant experience for all hikers. Please respect any posted local regulations regarding pets.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to access this area?
faqs.answer: No, the 'Callejones de Las Majadas' area, which this trail traverses, is a free-access natural park. You do not need a permit or to pay an entrance fee to enjoy this hike in the Valle de Vidriales.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this hike?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and varied terrain, it's advisable to bring sturdy hiking boots, sufficient water, snacks, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and layers of clothing suitable for changing weather conditions. A map or GPS device is also recommended, though the trail is typically well-signposted.
